coredata.dc:title: Highly fluorescent xerogels with entrapped carbon dots for organic scintillators
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:issueName:
European Materials Research Society (E-MRS) Spring Meeting 2013
Symposium O: Synthesis, processing and characterization of nanoscale multi functional oxide films IV
coredata.dc:creator: Quaranta, A.
Carturan, S.
Campagnaro, A.
Dalla Palma, M.
Giarola, M.
Daldosso, N.
Maggioni, G.
Mariotto, G.
coredata.dc:description: Organically modified silicate thin film and bulk samples were prepared using [3-(2-aminoethylamino)propyl]trimethoxysilane (AEAP-TMOS) as precursor with the addition of different amounts of AEAP-TMOS functionalized C-dots, prepared by reaction of AEAP-TMOS and citric acid at high temperature. The synthesis of surface functionalized C-dots was followed by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and the C-dots optical properties were characterized by optical absorption and UV–vis fluorescence. Thin xerogel films and bulk samples were studied by FTIR, Raman and fluorescence spectroscopy. Intense blue-green emission was observed by UV excitation of functionalized C-dots. Carbon quantum dot (CQD) luminescence was preserved also in the xerogel matrices, and the energy transfer from the matrix to CQDs, which is a key characteristic for scintillation detectors, was investigated in the two systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silica gel
Xerogel
Carbon quantum dots
Functionalization
FT-IR
Raman
Luminescence
Energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: CQD optical properties in xerogel matrices: bulk vs film

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial support by the Fifth Commission of the Istituto Nazionale di Fisica Nucleare (INFN) , HYDE experiment, is greatly acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption spectrum of CQD in ethanol solution and fluorescence emission at different excitation wavelengths.

Comparison between FTIR (a) and Raman (b) spectra of bulk and thin film matrices. In the inset of panel a, the FTIR spectra in the range between 1750cm−1 and 1600cm−1 of AEAP-TMOS precursor, AEAP-TMOS precursor containing glacial acetic acid, as dried bulk sample and a bulk sample after 10days in air, are also reported.
Xerogel luminescence emission spectra obtained exciting the sample at 290nm and at 360nm (solid lines). Dashed lines represent the respective excitation spectra with λem =360nm and λem =440nm.
Comparison between bulk (a) and film (b) emission spectra for different excitation wavelengths.
Excitation spectra for bulk, film and solution samples of the emissions recorded at 440nm (a) and 400nm (b).

head.ce:title: Highly fluorescent xerogels with entrapped carbon dots for organic scintillators

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Trento, Department of Industrial Engineering, via Mesiano, 77, 38123 Trento, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratori Nazionali di Legnaro, INFN, Viale dell'Università, 2, 35020 Legnaro (PD), Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Padova, Department of Physics and Astronomy “Galileo Galilei”, Via Marzolo, 8, 35131 Padova, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics and Astronomy “Galileo Galilei”

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Verona, Department of Informatics, Strada le Grazie,15, 37134 Verona, Italy

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: University of Trento, Department of Industrial Engineering, via Mesiano, 77, 38123 Trento, Italy. Tel.: +39 0461282450; fax: +39 0461 281945.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organically modified silicate thin film and bulk samples were prepared using [3-(2-aminoethylamino)propyl]trimethoxysilane (AEAP-TMOS) as precursor with the addition of different amounts of AEAP-TMOS functionalized C-dots, prepared by reaction of AEAP-TMOS and citric acid at high temperature. The synthesis of surface functionalized C-dots was followed by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and the C-dots optical properties were characterized by optical absorption and UV–vis fluorescence. Thin xerogel films and bulk samples were studied by FTIR, Raman and fluorescence spectroscopy. Intense blue-green emission was observed by UV excitation of functionalized C-dots. Carbon quantum dot (CQD) luminescence was preserved also in the xerogel matrices, and the energy transfer from the matrix to CQDs, which is a key characteristic for scintillation detectors, was investigated in the two systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a class of nanomaterials which recently attracted a great deal of attention owing to their optical properties ([1,2] and ref. therein). In fact, they exhibit a strong tunable luminescence in the visible region which can be exploited for a wide range of applications, like energy conversion systems, catalysis materials, bio-imaging, light emitting devices and photovoltaics. Moreover, CQDs are easy to synthesize following a variety of different procedures, classified as top-down and bottom-up approaches. Top-down methods consist of the breaking of large carbon-based systems using processes like arc discharge, laser ablation or electrochemical oxidation. Bottom-up approaches are based on treatments of molecular precursors whose decomposition gives rise to the formation of luminescent CQDs. Resulting dots are conjugated systems with a sp2 hybridization and with different arrangements of the carbon sheets, ranging from highly disordered graphite to amorphous carbon. Moreover, CQD surface can present different functional groups, that can be obtained in situ, during the synthesis of the C-dots themselves, as in the case of organic acids or carbohydrates decomposition, or ex situ, as a result of surface functionalization. In any case, the surface grafted chemical groups affect both the optical and the physico-chemical properties of these nanoparticles. In this framework, the origin of CQD optical properties is still under debate: in fact, at present, it is not clear if the luminescence features are due to quantum confinement effects of delocalized electrons, or to surface trapping sites or, even, to a combination of both the effects.

Though extensive work has been done on their synthesis and characterization, the entrapment of CQDs in oxide matrices for the production of functional systems is still at the early stages of development. Recently, Xie and co-workers [3,4] dispersed CQDs into a hybrid organic–inorganic sol–gel matrix following a two step route. Firstly, they produced functionalized CQD from the thermal decomposition of citric acid in presence of trialkoxysilanes with an amine terminated non-hydrolyzable group; thereafter, they linked the silane functionalized particles to a hybrid sol–gel matrix.

The linking to organic or hybrid networks opens a wide range of possibilities for the exploitation of CQD optical properties in different applications, ranging from luminescent systems for photovoltaics, like luminescent down-converters or luminescent solar concentrators, to scintillation radiation detectors. In particular, the possibility to use CQDs for the synthesis of new scintillators is quite intriguing, since it could allow to suitably exploit their radiation resistance in scintillation devices. In fact, CQDs are characterized by high photostability under laser irradiation, evidencing a good endurance owing to the highly delocalized structure [5,6].

In this work, CQDs have been dispersed into a hybrid sol–gel matrix following the procedure of Xie et al. for the production of both bulk and thin films samples in order to study their optical properties in the two types of systems. It is known that sol–gel thin films can give rise to changed network structures with respect to bulk systems, owing to the different evaporation rates and retention capability of both the solvents and the hydrolysis/condensation by-products [7]. The dissimilar behavior results in different kinds of interactions of the network with entrapped fluorescent compounds, like dye molecules. For instance, the dispersion of 3-hydroxyflavone in bulk and thin film hybrid systems evidenced how, in the latter, a more intimate contact with the matrix allowed the development of energy transfer mechanisms between the organic part of the network and the dye molecules [8].

In this work, fluorescence properties of CQDs in both bulk and thin film samples are studied in order to outline the occurrence of energy transfer processes between the dots and the matrix, which is a crucial aspect for the conversion of the energy released by ionizing radiation into light in scintillating materials [9].

Functionalized carbon quantum dots were synthetized following the procedure reported by Xie, Wang and co-workers [3,4]. In this synthesis, [3-(2-aminoethylamino)propyl]trimethoxysilane (AEAP-TMOS) was heated up to 240°C under Argon atmosphere. Citric acid, previously dehydrated in a vacuum oven at 100°C, was then added to the hot silane in a ratio of 50g per silane litre. The rapid decomposition of the organic acid in oxygen-free environment produces water vapor and, meanwhile, carbonaceous particles, whose residual carboxylic acid groups react with terminal NH2 giving rise to amide, which covalently bonds the carbon-rich phase with the alkoxy silane functionalities. The reaction was carried on for 1h in inert atmosphere, before cooling down. A mixture of toluene/hexane 1:1 was used to extract the CQD solution which was finally separated and diluted with acetone in 1:1000 proportion.

Xerogel samples were produced by acid catalyzed hydrolysis/condensation of AEAP-TMOS trialkoxysilane in absolute ethanol. AEAP-TMOS (1.6ml) was mixed with ethanol (about 3.2ml) and diluted (1M) hydrochloric acid (0.21ml), with a molar ratio silane:water:ethanol equal to 1:1.6:7.4. CQD acetone solution was added during this stage in 1% vol. ratio, before stirring for about 30min in air. Thereafter, thin film samples were produced by spin coating on silicon substrates at 1000rpm for 1min, while for the production of bulk samples, a suitable amount of solution was poured in polyethylene vials, sealed with punctured parafilm and left to dry for 3days in oven at 60°C. Film specimens resulted in approximately 8μm thin xerogel with a good adhesion to the substrate; bulk samples, after extraction from the vials, resulted in optically transparent disks about 700μm thick.

CQD surface functionalization and xerogel film and bulk samples structure were investigated by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy using a Jasco FTIR/660 plus spectrometer. The spectrum of as-synthesized CQDs was collected in transmission mode, by drying a drop of acetone solution of CQDs on a silicon substrate which was used as reference background. Citric acid was pelletized with anhydrous KBr and pure AEAP-TMOS was analyzed as a small drop between two KBr windows. This last method was also applied to follow the extent of the chemical reaction between citric acid and silane, by collecting a drop of mixture at different times. Finally, xerogel bulk samples were analyzed in attenuated total reflectance mode, using a ZnSe crystal with an incidence angle of 45°.

Raman spectra on both kinds of samples were carried out at room temperature in back-scattering geometry using a micro-Raman setup (Horiba Jobin-Yvon, model Labram HR), coupled to a He–Ne laser. The micro-probe head consisted of a long working distance and high numerical aperture (N.A.=0.75) 80× objective used to focus the laser beam on the sample and to collect the scattered radiation from it. The scattered radiation was filtered by a narrow band notch filter and dispersed by a spectrograph of 80cm focal length. The spectra were excited at 632.8nm with about 0.3mW power (measured at the sample surface) and detected by a CCD (1024×256pixels), cryogenically cooled by liquid nitrogen. The detection limit on the side of low wave-number, due to the notch filter, was about 200cm−1. The focused laser spot on the sample surface had an apparent diameter of about 1.5μm, and the spectral resolution was about 2cm−1.

UV–vis absorption spectra of CQDs were collected with a Jasco V-570 spectrophotometer using a 0.1% vol. diluted solution of the synthesis product in ethanol. The same solution was also used to measure CQD luminescence, in right angle geometry, by means of a Jasco FP-6300 spectrofluorimeter equipped with a 150W Xenon lamp. The same instrument was also used to record the fluorescence spectra of bulk and thin film samples in front face geometry.

Lifetime measurements were performed on a bulk xerogel sample with 0.2% CQD volume concentration excited by a nanoLED (Horiba Jobin-Yvon) at 360nm (pulse width of 1.1ns) and detected by a photomultiplier tube by using time-correlated single-photon counting technique.
Finally CQD quantum yield was measured by using an integrating sphere coupled to Horiba Scientific FluoroLog spectrofluorimeter.

Covalent grafting between the amine terminated silane and CQD carboxylic surface groups was confirmed by FTIR spectrum (not shown here), where the typical amide peak at 1654cm−1 [3,4,10] can be observed. It is also worth to notice the appearance of the typical components of SiOSi bridges (1120cm−1 and 1030cm−1) [11] in the spectrum of CQD solution, resulting from partial hydrolysis and condensation of Si-OCH3 groups upon exposure to humid air.

UV–vis absorption spectrum of as-synthesized CQD solution in acetone shows an absorption band ranging from 330nm to 400nm and centered around 360nm (Fig. 1 ), in good agreement with similarly functionalized carbon dots [3,4,12]. CQD excitation within this band leads to a strong luminescence feature, shown in Fig. 1, whose maximum intensity is centered at 450nm when excited at wavelengths up to 360nm, but it red shifts up to 470nm for increasing excitation wavelengths. This dependence of the emission spectrum position on the excitation wavelength has been previously reported [2,4,13] and it has been attributed to a size distribution and/or to a distribution of different emissive sites on the dot surface. It is however worth mentioning that the CQD luminescence process itself is not yet fully understood and further studies have to be done in this sense [1].

The luminescence time decay curve, obtained by exciting a CQD doped xerogel sample at 360nm and by detecting the emission intensity at 450nm was fitted with a double exponential function with time constants τ1 =1.6ns and τ2 =6.8ns, giving an average decay time τave =4.3ns. These results well agree with other works on the topic [1,2,13] and confirm the statement that different emissive sites are responsible of the luminescence features [1]. The measured CQD quantum yield for excitation al 360nm was about 26% for an absorbance value of 0.4, which is lower than similarly synthesized CQDs, using citric acid as a precursor and amine based capping agents [4,14], but much higher than CQDs synthesized following other procedures [1]. The observed difference from similar systems can be ascribed to a different size distribution or to different functionalization degrees of the CQD surface, but deeper inspections on both dimensions and structure of the produced dots are needed.

Concerning the Raman spectra, the only difference between the two types of samples, in the region below 1000cm−1 (Fig. 2b), is due to the occurrence of some structures (peak and bands) originating from the Si substrate underlying the film despite of the fact that its orientation was set in such a way to minimize the contribution of the scattering from it.

Undoped bulk xerogel samples exhibited two intrinsic luminescence features: one centered at 360nm, obtained by exciting the samples at 290nm, and the other one centered around 460nm, recorded at the excitation wavelength of 360nm (Fig. 3 ). The corresponding excitation spectra are peaked at 280 and 360nm, respectively. The intensity of both the emission bands was four times lower with respect to the sample containing CQDs.

The observed emission at 460nm has been already reported in different works on silica xerogels based on γ-aminopropyltriethoxysilanes employing organic acids [17,20,21], inorganic acids [21] or none [22] and even in papers on pure silica gels [23,24]. The band at 360nm was also previously observed [24,25] as well as its excitation peak around 280nm [21], but it is much less frequent, probably because it is hidden by the most intense luminescence from carbon related centers coming from organic acids [21]. There is a wide range of emissive species that can be responsible for both the luminescence features, but a certain attribution is still about to come [17,22,24,26]. It is a generally accepted hypothesis that carbon substitutional defects for Si can be responsible for luminescent centers in siliceous materials [17,21,22,26,27] and our hypothesis is that atmospheric carbon embedded in the silica network, evidenced by FTIR spectra, could play a role in the observed emission. More investigations have however to be done on this topic.

Luminescence from xerogel thin film samples is very weak and an exhaustive comparison with bulk is not possible. In any case, the position of the main emission features seems to be very close to that of monolithic samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQD optical properties in xerogel matrices: bulk vs film
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 4a,b the emission spectra of bulk and thin film samples are shown, obtained at different excitation wavelengths, ranging from 330 to 480nm. The spectra exhibit nearly the same behavior of CQDs in solution, but some differences both in shape and position of the peaks can be observed. By changing the excitation wavelength from 330nm to 360nm, in bulk samples the peak position is nearly constant at about 440nm, while in thin films it shifts from 423nm to 440nm. Furthermore, in the first spectrum of the thin specimen, a component at around 390 can be inferred from the peak shape. By increasing the excitation wavelength from 370 to 480nm, bulk samples are characterized by a shift of the emission peak up to 533nm due to the arising of a second component. On the other hand, in thin films, the peak shift is smaller (523nm) with a lower contribution of the band at wavelengths higher than 520nm.

The different behaviors can be related to the interactions of CQD with the embedding networks. CQD emission in bulk samples, excited at wavelength below 360nm, shows a more similar behavior to CQDs in solution, suggesting that dots are embedded in larger sites and surrounded by an environment comparable to the host solution. In thin films CQDs are more closely in contact with the network and this affects the luminescence features whose sensitivity on the environment is well known; this explains the wider shift noted at low excitation wavelengths. The more intimate contact with the matrix is due to the higher collapse, during drying, of thin film structures with respect to bulk samples. In fact, in thin films the solvent and water evaporation occurs within few seconds, with respect to the long times needed for sol gel monoliths, leaving very compliant weakly branched acid-catalyzed silicate species, which facilitate the compaction of the network [7,19].

The two additional components observed in the systems, the UV shoulder in thin films and the green band at 530nm in bulk samples can be related to the formation of different emitting species during network condensation and need further study. It is however worth mentioning that there is no such emission in the bulk matrix luminescence spectrum, even if the green band was already observed in similar materials [20,23] and in highly CQD doped gels [3].

The different kind of interaction between CQDs and organically modified silicate (ORMOSIL) network is further evidenced by observing the excitation spectra, shown in Fig. 5a,b. In particular, Fig. 5a reports the excitation spectra of CQD in bulk and thin film samples with λem =440nm, while Fig. 5b shows the excitation spectra of CQD in the two matrixes and in solution with λem =400nm. As it can be observed, bare CQD in solution does not show any excitation feature at wavelengths lower than 290nm, while embedded CQDs exhibit a peak at 265–270nm, which is much higher in thin film with respect to bulk sample. This excitation feature corresponds to excitation peak of the xerogel intrinsic emission at 360nm, shown in Fig. 3. The presence of the same peak in CQD excitation spectra is indicative of the occurrence of an energy transfer process between the matrix and the embedded dots, and higher is the energy transfer rate higher is the peak of the donor group in the excitation spectrum of the acceptor chromophore [8]. In particular, in thin film samples a greatly improved energy transfer from the matrix to CQDs is present with respect to bulk samples, owing to the closer interaction between CQDs and the matrix. The energy transfer between the host matrix and the embedded CQDs is the basis of the scintillation mechanism. For this reason, the systems studied in this work are promising candidates for the production of hybrid organic–inorganic scintillators for the detection of ionizing radiation.

Functionalized CQDs, characterized by a 26% QY and by an average decay time of 4.3ns, were synthesized by means of thermal decomposition of citric acid in presence of hot AEAP-TMOS. Thereafter they were dispersed into AEAP-TMOS based ORMOSIL bulk and thin film sol–gel samples. Vibrational spectroscopy analyses of condensed OROMOSILs showed a higher retention of NH3 + HCO3 groups in bulk samples, which can be related to the more difficult removal of volatile species with respect to thin films. Moreover, sol gel matrix is characterized by two luminescence features, at 360nm and 460nm, due to carbon related emitting centers. The spectra of embedded CQDs were slightly different from the collected spectra from CQDs in solution. In particular, in bulk samples, the response to different excitation wavelengths is more similar to that of CQD in solution, with the further addition of a second component around 530nm. On the other hand, in thin films, peaks obtained under excitation at lower wavelengths exhibit a higher shift and show a UV component arising around 390nm. The differences between the two systems can be related to the interaction between CQDs and matrices and further work is needed for an exhaustive characterization of the emitting centers. Moreover, as was shown by the excitation spectra, an energy transfer process occurs between the xerogel network and the CQDs making these systems suitable for the production of scintillators. In thin films the process is more efficient owing to a closer contact with the network related to the collapsing of the network during the fast evaporation of the solvent.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support by the Fifth Commission of the Istituto Nazionale di Fisica Nucleare (INFN), HYDE experiment, is greatly acknowledged.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Theory and Practice of Scintillation Counting
Infrared and Raman Characteristic Group Frequencies
